content.text: Integration tests verify that multiple components work correctly together — the HTTP route, database persistence, task queue, and agent orchestrator all wired as they would be in production. They occupy the middle layer of the testing pyramid: slower than unit tests but far more realistic, catching wiring bugs that isolated mocks can never expose.

An integration test exercises real boundaries between subsystems. Rather than mocking everything out, it spins up real infrastructure (or lightweight equivalents — a Postgres container, an in-memory Redis) and sends actual HTTP requests to your FastAPI application. The agent orchestrator, tool dispatch layer, DB writes, and queue enqueues all run as they would in production. Only the external LLM API and third-party services are stubbed, because those are expensive, slow, and non-deterministic.

The boundary is deliberate: unit tests verify that individual functions behave correctly in isolation; integration tests verify that those functions remain correct when plugged together through all the real wiring — dependency injection, async event loops, middleware, serialization, and database constraints.

A perfectly unit-tested tool handler can still fail in the API when dependency injection is misconfigured, an async context variable is missing, or the SQL model has a constraint the unit test never triggered. A poorly serialized Pydantic model might pass mypy and all unit tests but raise a 422 under real HTTP traffic. Integration tests catch these wiring bugs before deployment.

For agentic systems in particular, the surface area between components is large: the orchestrator depends on an LLM stub response to determine which tool to call, the tool writes to a DB row, and the polling endpoint must read that row consistently. Integration tests give you confidence that these multi-hop chains work end-to-end without spending money on real LLM calls.
— wrong HTTP status codes, missing headers, DB constraint violations, and queue serialization errors all surface here
— the OpenAPI response shape your frontend or downstream service expects is tested against the actual implementation, not a hand-written mock
— refactoring dependency injection or moving a module import cannot silently break the API; the test will fail immediately
— a Postgres or Redis container behaves like the real service in almost all respects, unlike in-memory fakes that paper over differences
— authentication middleware, CORS headers, and rate-limit counters only activate under real HTTP flows; integration tests exercise all of them
— server-sent event endpoints cannot be verified by unit tests; only a real HTTP stream exposes chunked-transfer issues
— spinning up containers and running migrations adds minutes; not suitable for the tight red-green cycle of TDD
— shared state across tests, port conflicts, and timing-sensitive async code make integration suites more fragile than unit suites
— requires Docker or a CI environment that can run postgres and redis containers; local dev setup is heavier
— the LLM is stubbed; answer correctness and hallucination rate still require eval suites run against real models
— a failing integration test may require inspecting DB rows, queue messages, and HTTP logs simultaneously

The decision rule is: stub at the external boundary, use real or containerized infrastructure for everything you own. The LLM API (Anthropic, OpenAI) is mocked at the HTTP client level because it is non-deterministic and paid. Everything else — your FastAPI routes, SQLAlchemy models, Redis writes, Celery task enqueue in eager mode — runs for real.
FastAPI app, all routes and middleware, Postgres DB (containerized), Redis (containerized), Celery in ALWAYS_EAGER mode
LLM HTTP client, third-party search APIs, email/notification services, external OAuth endpoints

Tests that share a database accumulate state and become order-dependent. The two standard strategies are: (1) transaction rollback — each test opens a connection, begins a transaction, runs all DB operations inside it, then rolls back after the test; (2) table truncation — truncate all tables in a fixture that runs before each test. Transaction rollback is faster and preferred when the ORM session can be bound to an existing connection; truncation is necessary when the code under test opens its own connections (common with async drivers).

Stub the LLM at the lowest practical boundary — the HTTP client method, not a high-level wrapper. Patching the wrapper risks missing the serialization code between your wrapper and the real HTTP call. Return a fixed dict that matches the real API response shape exactly; this way, any schema change in your deserialization code will break the integration test even though no real call was made.

If the orchestrator is async, use httpx.AsyncClient wrapped in pytest-asyncio rather than FastAPI's synchronous TestClient. For Celery tasks, set CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=True in the test config; tasks execute synchronously inline instead of sending to a worker, keeping test runs self-contained without a live worker process.
The diagram below shows the full integration test setup. Solid arrows represent real boundaries the tests traverse; the dashed arrow to the LLM Stub shows the one mocked external call.
— any route that reads or writes to the DB, enqueues a task, or returns a specific status code
— incoming webhook events that trigger agent runs need a real HTTP post to exercise the parsing and dispatch path
— server-sent event and streaming responses that cannot be verified by inspecting a function's return value alone
— confirming that POST /runs enqueues a task with the correct payload and that the task can deserialize and run it
— foreign key relationships, unique constraints, and cascade deletes that only fire under real SQL
— middleware that runs before route handlers is invisible to unit tests; integration tests exercise the full request pipeline
Do not use integration tests for: prompt quality (use eval suites), pure algorithmic logic (use unit tests), or performance benchmarks (use load testing).
— HTTP + DB + queue tests, requires containers
— shared fixtures: engine, session, client, mock_llm, auth_headers
— postgres and redis service definitions for CI
— marker registration for integration and unit

The fixture chain is: session-scoped table creation, function-scoped transaction-wrapped session, function-scoped TestClient with DI override, and function-scoped LLM patch. Each test therefore starts from a clean DB state without truncating tables, because the transaction is rolled back.

The most important integration test for an agent service covers the full lifecycle: create a run, execute it, and verify that tool-call events were persisted. The LLM stub returns a fixed tool_call response; the integration test asserts that the orchestrator dispatched that tool and the event row exists in the DB.

# tests/integration/test_run_lifecycle.py
import pytest

pytestmark = pytest.mark.integration


def test_create_run_returns_202_and_persists(client, mock_llm, auth_headers):
    """POST /v1/runs creates a DB row and returns run_id."""
    resp = client.post(
        "/v1/runs",
        json={"prompt": "find recent papers on agentic AI"},
        headers=auth_headers,
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 202
    body = resp.json()
    assert "run_id" in body
    run_id = body["run_id"]

    # Confirm the row is readable
    status_resp = client.get(f"/v1/runs/{run_id}", headers=auth_headers)
    assert status_resp.status_code == 200
    data = status_resp.json()
    assert data["status"] in ("queued", "running", "completed")
    assert data["prompt"] == "find recent papers on agentic AI"


def test_unauthenticated_request_returns_401(client):
    """Auth middleware blocks requests with no token."""
    resp = client.post("/v1/runs", json={"prompt": "hello"})
    assert resp.status_code == 401


def test_tool_call_events_recorded(client, mock_llm, auth_headers, db_session):
    """Executing a run persists tool_call events to the DB."""
    resp = client.post(
        "/v1/runs",
        json={"prompt": "search for news"},
        headers=auth_headers,
    )
    run_id = resp.json()["run_id"]

    # Run synchronously via test helper endpoint
    client.post(f"/v1/runs/{run_id}/execute", headers=auth_headers)

    events_resp = client.get(f"/v1/runs/{run_id}/events", headers=auth_headers)
    assert events_resp.status_code == 200
    events = events_resp.json()["events"]
    tool_events = [e for e in events if e["type"] == "tool_call"]
    assert len(tool_events) >= 1
    assert tool_events[0]["tool_name"] == "web_search"


def test_streaming_endpoint_returns_event_stream(client, mock_llm, auth_headers):
    """GET /v1/runs/{id}/stream returns text/event-stream with data: lines."""
    create_resp = client.post(
        "/v1/runs", json={"prompt": "stream test"}, headers=auth_headers
    )
    run_id = create_resp.json()["run_id"]

    with client.stream("GET", f"/v1/runs/{run_id}/stream", headers=auth_headers) as resp:
        assert resp.status_code == 200
        ct = resp.headers.get("content-type", "")
        assert "text/event-stream" in ct
        lines = list(resp.iter_lines())
        assert any(line.startswith("data:") for line in lines)


def test_rate_limit_returns_429(client, mock_llm, auth_headers):
    """Exceed per-key rate limit and expect 429 Too Many Requests."""
    for _ in range(10):
        client.post("/v1/runs", json={"prompt": "test"}, headers=auth_headers)
    final = client.post("/v1/runs", json={"prompt": "test"}, headers=auth_headers)
    assert final.status_code == 429
    assert "retry_after" in final.json()

A production agent service at a fintech company added an integration test suite after a silent regression: a refactor moved the Celery task import path, which broke task discovery in production but all unit tests passed because they mocked the task directly. The integration test — which ran the full POST /v1/runs path with Celery in eager mode — would have caught the ImportError immediately.

Their CI pipeline: GitHub Actions spins up the docker-compose.test.yml stack via a service container, runs migrations against the test DB, then runs pytest with -m integration. The suite takes four minutes total and runs on every PR. Unit tests run in parallel in a separate job and finish in twelve seconds. LLM eval suites run nightly against the real API on a scheduled workflow.
— tests that insert rows without rollback or truncation become order-dependent; the third test passes only because the first inserted the row it reads
— forgetting to patch the LLM client means integration tests make real API calls, incurring cost and non-determinism
— without @pytest.mark.integration the slow suite runs alongside unit tests on every file save in a local TDD loop
— the stub returns fixed text; asserting exact wording is brittle even against a stub and teaches nothing about real quality
— connecting tests to the production database risks data corruption and constraint violations that are very hard to undo
— if you mock the DB session inside an integration test you have written a unit test with extra steps; let the real DB run
— asserting the Postgres container is up by sleep(2) is fragile; use healthcheck-based wait-for-it logic or testcontainers lifecycle hooks
on every test in the integration folder; configure pytest.ini so the marker is registered and the suite is easy to skip locally
— bind the test session to a rolled-back transaction rather than truncating; it is ten times faster for suites with many DB writes
Stub the LLM at the HTTP layer
— patch the lowest-level HTTP call, not a wrapper class; this ensures deserialization code is still exercised
— set CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER=True in test settings so tasks run inline without a worker; keeps the test self-contained
— check HTTP status codes, response schema fields, DB row counts, and queue message payloads; never check LLM prose
Record cassettes only for stable APIs
— VCR cassettes freeze third-party API responses; update them when the upstream schema changes, or they will hide real integration bugs
Run integration in CI on every PR
— integration is the right place to catch merge-time wiring regressions before they reach staging
Keep containers alive across the session
— start postgres and redis once per pytest session, not per test; use scope='session' on the engine fixture
— unit tests are safe to parallelize with pytest-xdist; integration tests share containers and often a DB, so run them serially or use isolated schemas per worker
— if you must parallelize integration tests, give each worker its own Postgres schema (SEARCH_PATH) so transactions never conflict
— create the engine and apply migrations once per pytest session with scope='session'; per-test startup adds minutes at scale
— designate a subset of fast integration tests (auth check, health endpoint, single happy-path run) as a smoke suite; run smoke on every commit, full suite nightly or pre-merge
— avoids docker-compose entirely; containers start and stop automatically as pytest fixtures, simplifying CI configuration
Markers, parametrize, fixtures with scope control
Sync HTTP client for FastAPI apps
Async HTTP client for async routes
Use with pytest-asyncio for async test functions
Required for async def test_ functions
Session fixture enables transaction rollback isolation
Starts Postgres/Redis as Python context managers
Brings up compose stack as a session fixture
Generates SQLAlchemy model instances for fixtures
Pair with integration run to measure realistic coverage
Freeze third-party API responses for stable playback

Integration tests occupy the sweet spot between unit speed and eval fidelity. They validate that your code is wired correctly without the cost and non-determinism of real LLM calls. Use all four layers: unit for logic, integration for wiring, eval for quality, and end-to-end for user-facing flows.

Integration tests need real DB credentials and may need API keys for mocked services. Store these as CI secrets (GitHub Actions secrets, not repository variables), inject them as environment variables, and never print them in test output. Use a dedicated test database user with only the permissions the application needs — not a superuser.

Never run integration tests against a production database. Use a separate test database, either in a container or a dedicated test cluster. If your team shares a staging database, use per-developer schemas to avoid collisions. After each test run, the rollback-based isolation strategy leaves the DB in the same state it was before — there is no cleanup step to forget.

When the LLM is stubbed, integration tests cannot accidentally consume tokens. But if any integration test reaches a real third-party service (a webhook endpoint, a search API), use environment variable flags (INTEGRATION_REAL_APIS=0 by default) to gate those calls. Without this gate, a developer who accidentally runs the integration suite locally with real credentials may consume quota or pollute external state.

A flaky integration test is almost always a symptom of a real problem: a timing assumption in async code, a missing database constraint, or a race condition in the queue. Investigate every flake rather than adding retry logic. The pytest-rerunfailures plugin can mask the signal if used too liberally.

Integration tests are the quality check on your agent's assembly line. Unit tests verify that each part is machined to spec. Integration tests verify that the parts fit together correctly when bolted to the frame. The LLM is the engine — you stub it so you can test the drivetrain without needing to run the engine every time.
Integration tests verify multi-component wiring: HTTP route → orchestrator → DB/queue
Use FastAPI TestClient or httpx.AsyncClient; bind to a transaction-rolled-back DB session for isolation
Stub the LLM at the HTTP boundary; run Postgres and Redis as real containers
Mark tests with @pytest.mark.integration; run them in CI on every PR, not every unit-test loop
Assert HTTP status codes, response schema, DB side effects, and queue payloads — never LLM prose
Complement with eval suites for answer quality; use end-to-end tests for user-facing flows
A flaky integration test signals a real code problem; investigate rather than retry

# docker-compose.test.yml — spin up test dependencies for CI
# Usage: docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up -d
#        pytest tests/integration -m integration --tb=short
#        docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml down

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: testdb
      POSTGRES_USER: test
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U test"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

# pytest.ini — marker registration avoids PytestUnknownMarkWarning
# [pytest]
# markers =
#     integration: slow tests requiring live DB and Redis
#     unit: pure-logic tests with no I/O dependencies
#
# Run only unit tests locally:   pytest -m unit
# Run full suite in CI:          pytest -m "unit or integration"
